title: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: where this is going

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: where this is going
We'll close with a look at the frontier.
Five threads worth watching.
One: antibody-based conditioning replacing busulfan.
I covered this in Segment 14 — anti-CD117 ADCs, anti-CD45 antibodies — that can deplete HSCs from their niches without the systemic toxicity of chemotherapy.
Outpatient conditioning, no infertility, no mucositis, dramatically lower treatment-related mortality.
This would transform every application of HSCT, especially pediatric gene therapy.
Two: CAR-HSC for in vivo CAR-T generation.
Instead of harvesting T cells, editing them, and reinfusing — what if you could edit a patient's HSCs to produce CAR-T cells continuously, for the rest of their life?
The HSCs would differentiate into T cells expressing the CAR, providing durable immunosurveillance.
Multiple groups are exploring this.
The combination of HSC editing and CAR design represents a deep integration of two cellular medicines.
Three: AAV6 and other vectors for in situ HSC editing.
Modern gene therapy uses ex vivo editing — pull the cells out, edit them, put them back.
The next step is in vivo editing — deliver an editing reagent directly to HSCs in the patient's body, no harvest required.
AAV6 has shown some HSC tropism.
Lipid nanoparticles targeting HSCs are in development.
If this works, it eliminates the manufacturing burden of cellular therapy and dramatically reduces cost.
Four: the economics of sickle cell cure.
Casgevy and Lyfgenia are priced at over $2 million per patient.
The US has approximately 100,000 sickle cell patients.
The arithmetic — $200 billion to cure the entire US sickle cell population — is genuinely staggering.

The combination of antibody-based conditioning, manufacturing automation, and eventual in vivo editing has to bring this cost down by an order of magnitude or two for these cures to be deliverable at population scale.
This is, ultimately, an engineering problem; it is also a policy problem.
Five: the convergence with other cellular therapies.
Allogeneic universal donor HSCs, gene-edited to evade host immunity.
Off-the-shelf cellular products built on cord blood or iPSC-derived HSCs.
Combinations of HSCT with checkpoint inhibitors, with BiTEs, with TIL therapy.
The boundaries between transplant, gene therapy, and immunotherapy are dissolving.
The chassis stays.
The payloads multiply.
So let me leave you with this.
Hematopoietic stem cell transplant is one of the oldest, most consequential, most underrated procedures in modern medicine.
It cured the first leukemia patients.
It established the concept that you can take cells from one person and rebuild a vital organ system in another.
It demonstrated for the first time that immunology — graft-versus-tumor — could be harnessed as a cancer therapy.
It built the regulatory, clinical, and manufacturing infrastructure on which CAR-T, gene therapy, and the next generation of cellular medicines run.
E.
Donnall Thomas and his colleagues built a chassis.
We are still, sixty years later, putting new things on it.
That's where we'll stop.
Thanks for your attention.

Next week we'll pick up with gene therapy in solid organs — the AAV revolution, the eye, the muscle, the brain — and we'll see how the HSCT model translates, and how it doesn't, to tissues that don't have a built-in homing mechanism.
